article_title: Role of Whole-Body Computed Tomography Scan to Avoid Missed Foreign Body in Patients with Multiple Stab Injury: A Rare Case of Retained Impaled Knife Blade with Intact Neurology
label: Fig. 1
caption: Computed tomography cervical spine: ( A , B ) Sagittal images showing the path of impaled knife passing through C5 lamina, across the spinal canal onto C6 vertebral body. ( C ) Axial image showing knife resting over C5 vertebral body passing through the canal.

article_title: Role of Whole-Body Computed Tomography Scan to Avoid Missed Foreign Body in Patients with Multiple Stab Injury: A Rare Case of Retained Impaled Knife Blade with Intact Neurology
label: Fig. 2
caption: Intraoperative images showing ( A ) incised wound over the posterior aspect of the neck with a visible broken knife blade below the skin, ( B ) wound exploration around knife blade that was passing through the inferior part of C5 lamina, ( C ) status of dura after closure.

article_title: Role of Whole-Body Computed Tomography Scan to Avoid Missed Foreign Body in Patients with Multiple Stab Injury: A Rare Case of Retained Impaled Knife Blade with Intact Neurology
label: Fig. 3
caption: Postoperative magnetic resonance imaging: ( A ) Sagittal T2-weighted sequence with normal cord without evidence of cerebrospinal fluid leak and ( B ) diffusion tensor imaging tractography with intact tract continuity and thickness.
